chapter_summary: The chapter begins with sages approaching Manu, who is seated in meditation, and requesting him to teach the dharmas of all varṇas and those born between them. Manu agrees and narrates the creation of the universe: from primordial darkness, the self-existent Lord (Svayambhū) manifests, creates waters, deposits a seed that becomes a golden egg, from which Brahmā is born. Brahmā divides the egg into heaven and earth, creates the mind, ego, the five great elements, the senses, and then assigns names, actions, and the Vedas. He also creates time, celestial bodies, rivers, oceans, mountains, and various emotions.

story.title: Manu's Discourse on Creation
story.arc: Cosmogony
story.timeline_note: Primordial creation before human history.
story.comic_potential: The golden egg and the division of the universe could be depicted visually.

teachings.teaching: The universe originates from the self-existent Lord who manifests from darkness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the initial state of pure consciousness (avyakta) from which the yogi must emerge through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The creation of mind, ego, and elements forms the basis of all existence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the microcosmic creation within the yogi's own being, where the mind and senses must be controlled to realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas are the eternal source of dharma, revealed at creation.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma, the Vedas represent the inner sound (nāda) that the yogi hears in deep meditation, guiding the path to liberation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti describes the creation of the universe by the self-existent Lord (Svayambhu). He creates speech, desire, anger, and then distinguishes dharma and adharma. He divides his body into male and female, from whom Virāj is born. Virāj performs austerity and creates Manu, who then creates the ten great sages (Marīci, Atri, Aṅgiras, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, Pracetas, Vasiṣṭha, Bhṛgu, Nārada). These sages create the seven Manus, gods, celestial beings, and all creatures. The chapter also classifies living beings into four types: jarāyuja (viviparous), aṇḍaja (oviparous), svedaja (moisture-born), and udbhijja (seed-born).

story.title: Creation of the Universe
story.arc: Cosmogony
story.timeline_note: Primordial creation sequence before the current Manvantara.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic genesis with divine beings emerging from the self-existent Lord, suitable for epic visual storytelling.

teachings.teaching: The Lord creates the world through tapas (austerity) and assigns each being its inherent nature and karma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that one's essential nature (svabhava) is determined by past actions and can be transformed through disciplined practice (kriya).

teachings.teaching: The four varnas (Brāhmaṇa, Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, Śūdra) originate from the mouth, arms, thighs, and feet of the Lord, respectively.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a metaphor for the functional differentiation of human society based on innate tendencies, which can be harmonized through yoga.

teachings.teaching: All living beings are classified into four birth types: viviparous, oviparous, moisture-born, and seed-born.
teachings.lens_note: This classification underscores the diversity of life forms, all subject to the law of karma, which Kriya-Yoga seeks to transcend through inner purification.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Manusmriti describes the cosmic cycle of creation and dissolution, the nature of time, and the lineage of Manus. It explains how the supreme being creates and withdraws the universe through waking and sleeping states, and details the divisions of time from moments to the ages of the gods.

story.title: The Cosmic Cycles and the Manvantaras
story.arc: Exposition of time and creation
story.timeline_note: Describes the beginning of creation and the succession of Manus.
story.comic_potential: The description of Brahma's waking and sleeping as cosmic cycles could be visualized as a giant being breathing the universe in and out.

teachings.teaching: The universe is created and dissolved through the waking and sleeping of the supreme being.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practitioner's conscious control of prana and awareness, where waking (jagrat) and sleeping (sushupti) states are integrated through meditation to realize the cosmic cycles within.

teachings.teaching: Time is divided into units from moments to the ages of the gods, with the four yugas decreasing in length.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views these time cycles as internal rhythms of breath and consciousness; the decreasing lengths of yugas reflect the progressive contraction of awareness in the kali yuga, which can be reversed through yogic practices.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Manusmriti describes the cosmic cycle of creation and dissolution, the nature of time (yugas), the decline of dharma across the four ages, the duties of the four varnas, and the supremacy of the Brahmana as the highest being, born from the mouth of Brahma.

story.title: The Cosmic Creation and the Hierarchy of Beings
story.arc: Exposition of the cyclical nature of time, the decline of dharma, and the establishment of varna duties.
story.timeline_note: Describes the beginning of a new kalpa and the subsequent yugas.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic contrast between the perfect Krita Yuga and the degraded Kali Yuga, with the Brahmana as the central figure, could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The day of Brahma lasts a thousand yugas, and his night is of equal length. At the end of the night, he awakens and creates the world.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the cycle of prana (cosmic energy) expanding and contracting. The awakening corresponds to the rise of kundalini, and the night to its rest.

teachings.teaching: From the mind of Brahma, when he desires to create, the subtle elements (ether, air, fire, water, earth) arise in sequence, each with its specific quality (sound, touch, form, taste, smell).
teachings.lens_note: This sequence mirrors the internal process of Kriya-Yoga: from the subtle vibration of Om (ether) to the gross physical sensations (earth). The practitioner can reverse this process to transcend the elements.

teachings.teaching: In Krita Yuga, dharma is complete and four-footed; in each subsequent yuga, it loses one foot due to theft, falsehood, and deceit.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the gradual loss of spiritual awareness. In Kriya-Yoga, the 'four feet' correspond to the four states of consciousness (waking, dreaming, deep sleep, turiya). The decline represents the dominance of the lower states.

teachings.teaching: The duties of the four varnas are prescribed: Brahmins teach and study, Kshatriyas protect, Vaishyas trade and farm, Shudras serve.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these duties are internalized as the four functions of the mind: intellect (Brahmin), will (Kshatriya), desire (Vaishya), and service (Shudra). The goal is to harmonize them.

teachings.teaching: The Brahmana is the highest being, born from the mouth of Brahma, and is the lord of all creation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as the supremacy of the spiritual intellect (buddhi) over the senses and mind. The 'mouth' symbolizes the source of speech and mantra, central to Kriya-Yoga practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 1.97-2.2) discusses the supreme status of Brahmins, the importance of conduct (ācāra) as the root of dharma, and outlines the contents of the entire Manusmriti text. It emphasizes that a Brahmin is born as the embodiment of dharma, and that proper conduct is essential for attaining the fruits of Vedic knowledge. The chapter also lists the various topics covered in the Manusmriti, including creation, sacraments, marriage, duties of kings, and more.

story.title: The Glory of Brahmins and the Importance of Conduct
story.arc: Exposition of Brahminical supremacy and the scope of Manusmriti
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Manusmriti, traditionally attributed to Svayambhuva Manu, and is set in a timeless didactic context.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a dense legal and philosophical text with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmin is born as the embodiment of dharma and is the lord of all beings for the protection of the treasury of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the inherent potential for self-realization and the duty to uphold righteousness through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: Conduct (ācāra) is the supreme dharma; without it, a Brahmin does not attain the fruit of the Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the necessity of ethical and ritual purity as a foundation for yogic practice, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: The Manusmriti covers all aspects of dharma for the four varnas, including conduct, duties, and purification.
teachings.lens_note: This comprehensive scope mirrors the holistic approach of Kriya-Yoga, which integrates all aspects of life into spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 2, verses 2-26) discusses the foundations of dharma, emphasizing the role of desire (kāma) in Vedic study and ritual action, the sources of dharma (Veda, Smṛti, good conduct, and self-satisfaction), the importance of following śruti and smṛti, the definition of sacred regions (Brahmāvarta, Madhyadeśa, Āryāvarta), and the necessity of performing saṃskāras for the twice-born.

story.title: Foundations of Dharma and Sacred Geography
story.arc: Exposition
story.timeline_note: Timeless; Manu's instructions to sages.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text.

teachings.teaching: Desire (kāma) is the root of Vedic study and ritual action; all actions are motivated by desire.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the principle that intentional action (kriya) must be guided by a purified desire (kāma) to align with dharma, not by selfish attachment.

teachings.teaching: The four sources of dharma are Veda, Smṛti, good conduct of the virtuous, and self-satisfaction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Self-satisfaction (ātmanas tuṣṭi) corresponds to inner discernment through yogic practice, where the purified mind intuits dharma.

teachings.teaching: One should follow śruti and smṛti; rejecting them leads to being an outcast.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The authority of śruti and smṛti is upheld as the foundation for kriya-yoga practice, ensuring alignment with tradition.

teachings.teaching: The land where the black antelope naturally roams is fit for sacrifice; beyond is mleccha territory.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This geographical purity symbolizes the need for a conducive environment for spiritual practice (kriya-yoga).

teachings.teaching: Twice-born must undergo saṃskāras from conception to cremation for purification.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Saṃskāras are kriyas that purify the body and mind, preparing the individual for higher yogic practices.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Manusmriti outlines the prescribed sacraments (saṃskāras) for the twice-born (dvija) castes, starting from conception (niṣeka) through the upanayana (sacred thread ceremony). It details the purificatory rites, naming ceremony, first outing, first feeding, tonsure, and the timing and materials for the sacred thread and staff, emphasizing the importance of timely performance to avoid becoming a vrātya (outcaste).

story.title: The Saṃskāras for the Twice-Born
story.arc: Prescriptive exposition of rites of passage
story.timeline_note: Timeless legal code; no narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic legal text.

teachings.teaching: The sacraments (saṃskāras) from conception onward purify the body and ensure spiritual merit both here and hereafter.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these external rites symbolize inner purification through disciplined action (kriya), aligning with the principle that physical rituals prepare the body for higher spiritual practices.

teachings.teaching: Timely performance of upanayana is crucial; delay leads to loss of caste status (vrātya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that initiation (upanayana) corresponds to the awakening of the spiritual self; missing the window signifies a missed opportunity for yogic rebirth, reinforcing the urgency of disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The materials for the sacred thread and staff vary by caste, reflecting different duties and spiritual paths.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these external distinctions mirror the internal vāsanās (tendencies) that must be purified; the specific materials symbolize the unique kriyas suited to each temperament.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 2, verses 50-74) provides detailed instructions on the daily practices of a brahmin student (brahmachari), including rules for begging alms, eating, purification (achamana), the sacred thread (upavita), and the procedure for approaching the guru for study. It emphasizes purity, respect for food, and the importance of the guru.

story.title: Daily Conduct of a Brahmachari
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Timeless; part of the dharmaśāstra tradition.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily prescriptive rules.

teachings.teaching: One should eat facing east for longevity, south for fame, west for prosperity, north for truth.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, directional eating aligns the practitioner with cosmic energies, enhancing the flow of prana during digestion.

teachings.teaching: Food should be honored and never criticized; honored food gives strength and energy, dishonored food destroys both.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the attitude of gratitude transforms food into prasadam, elevating consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Overeating is unhealthy, shortens life, and is not conducive to heaven; it should be avoided.
teachings.lens_note: Moderation in diet is a key Kriya-Yoga practice to maintain sattva and support meditation.

teachings.teaching: A brahmin should perform achamana (sipping water) with the 'brahma tirtha' (base of thumb), not with the 'pitrya tirtha' (finger tips).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that precise ritual actions purify the subtle body and prepare it for higher practices.

teachings.teaching: The guru should be approached with folded hands (brahmanjali) and touched appropriately; at the beginning and end of study, the guru's feet should be grasped.
teachings.lens_note: This embodies surrender and transmission of knowledge; in Kriya-Yoga, the guru's grace is essential for spiritual awakening.

teachings.teaching: The syllable Om should be uttered at the beginning and end of Vedic study; without it, knowledge slips away.
teachings.lens_note: Om is the primordial vibration; in Kriya-Yoga, chanting Om aligns the practitioner with the cosmic sound, facilitating inner stillness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 2, verses 74-98) discusses the importance of the sacred syllable Om, the Gayatri mantra, pranayama, and control of the senses. It emphasizes the superiority of japa (silent repetition) over ritual sacrifices, and describes the eleven senses (five buddhindriyas, five karmendriyas, and mind). The text teaches that sense control leads to spiritual success.

story.title: The Discipline of Pranava and Sense Control
story.arc: Exposition on spiritual practices: Om, Gayatri, pranayama, and sense restraint lead to liberation.
story.timeline_note: Timeless teachings; no narrative sequence.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: The syllable Om should be uttered at the beginning and end of Vedic recitation; without it, the recitation is incomplete.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, Om is the primordial vibration that aligns the practitioner with cosmic consciousness, essential for any spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: One should perform pranayama three times before reciting Om, purifying the body and mind.
teachings.lens_note: Pranayama in Kriya-Yoga is a technique to control prana and still the mind, preparing for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Gayatri mantra (Savitri) is extracted from the three Vedas and is the mouth of Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Gayatri is seen as the essence of all mantras, and its repetition with understanding leads to spiritual illumination.

teachings.teaching: Regular recitation of the Gayatri with the three vyahritis (bhur, bhuvah, svah) for three years leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Consistent practice (abhyasa) is key in Kriya-Yoga; the three years symbolize the gradual purification of the three bodies (physical, astral, causal).

teachings.teaching: Japa (silent repetition) is ten times superior to ritual sacrifices; whispered japa is a hundred times better; mental japa is a thousand times better.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes internal practice over external rituals; mental japa aligns with the inner Kriya-Yoga technique of subtle vibration.

teachings.teaching: The senses are eleven: five buddhindriyas (hearing, touch, sight, taste, smell) and five karmendriyas (speech, hands, feet, anus, genitals) plus the mind as the eleventh.
teachings.lens_note: Control of the mind is paramount; in Kriya-Yoga, the mind is the gateway to the inner self, and its mastery leads to control of all senses.

teachings.teaching: Desire is never quenched by enjoyment; it increases like fire with ghee.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that sense indulgence strengthens desires; only through inner discipline (kriya) can one transcend them.

teachings.teaching: Renunciation of all desires is superior to attaining all objects of desire.
teachings.lens_note: True renunciation is internal; in Kriya-Yoga, one renounces attachment while performing actions, not the actions themselves.

teachings.teaching: One who is not elated or depressed by sensory experiences is known as jitendriya (conqueror of the senses).
teachings.lens_note: Equanimity is a sign of a realized yogi; Kriya-Yoga aims at this state through steady meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 2, verses 98-122) discusses self-control, the importance of the Sandhyavandana (twilight prayers), the guru-disciple relationship, and the proper conduct of a brahmachari (student). It emphasizes mastery over the senses, the necessity of daily Vedic study, and the respect due to teachers.

story.title: Rules for a Brahmachari
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Part of the Dharmaśāstra tradition, not a narrative story.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of rules and aphorisms.

teachings.teaching: One who does not rejoice or despair upon hearing, touching, seeing, eating, or smelling is known as a conqueror of the senses.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this indicates the state of pratyahara (sense withdrawal), where the yogi remains unaffected by sensory inputs, essential for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: If even one sense is uncontrolled, wisdom leaks away like water from a cracked pot.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that in Kriya-Yoga, each sense must be brought under control through specific techniques (e.g., trataka for eyes, pranayama for nose) to prevent pranic leakage.

teachings.teaching: One should perform the Sandhyavandana (twilight prayers) standing in the morning and sitting in the evening, reciting the Savitri mantra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets Sandhyavandana as a daily practice of aligning with cosmic rhythms, using mantra and breath to harmonize the pranic body.

teachings.teaching: A twice-born who neglects the Sandhyavandana is to be excluded from all Vedic duties like a Shudra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that regularity in spiritual practice (sadhana) is non-negotiable; skipping it leads to spiritual decline.

teachings.teaching: One should study the Veda daily; there is no prohibition on study for the daily Vedic recitation, as it is a continuous sacrifice to Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views daily Vedic study as a form of japa-yoga, maintaining a constant connection to the divine sound vibration.

teachings.teaching: A student should serve his guru, collect alms, sleep on the ground, and do what is beneficial for the guru until he returns home after completing his studies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights the guru-shishya parampara as essential for transmitting Kriya-Yoga techniques; service purifies the mind and builds humility.

teachings.teaching: One should not speak unless asked, and even if knowledgeable, should behave like a fool in worldly matters.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma advises that a yogi should remain silent (mauna) to conserve energy and avoid unnecessary distractions, speaking only when it serves dharma.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge should not be taught to one who is envious or impure; it should be given to a pure, self-controlled brahmachari who guards it like a treasure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma insists that Kriya-Yoga initiations are only for qualified students who have proven their sincerity and purity, to protect the sacred teachings.

teachings.teaching: One who learns the Veda without the guru's permission is guilty of stealing the Veda and goes to hell.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma underscores the necessity of initiation (diksha) in Kriya-Yoga; self-taught practices can be dangerous and ineffective.

teachings.teaching: A brahmana who is self-controlled, even if only knowing the Savitri mantra, is superior to an uncontrolled one who knows the three Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma values practical realization over theoretical knowledge; a simple practitioner with inner control is more advanced than a learned but undisciplined scholar.

teachings.teaching: One should rise and greet an elder; by doing so, one regains the pranas that would otherwise depart.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma explains that respect for elders aligns the pranic flow; the act of bowing activates the spine and honors the lineage.

teachings.teaching: A brahmana greeting a superior should state his name and say 'I am so-and-so'.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a practice of humility and self-identity, reminding the practitioner of their place in the spiritual hierarchy.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 2.122-2.146) provides detailed rules for respectful greetings (abhivādana) and hierarchical relationships among brāhmaṇas, kṣatriyas, vaiśyas, and śūdras. It specifies how to address elders, teachers, and relatives, and emphasizes the superiority of the spiritual teacher (ācārya) over biological parents.

story.title: Rules of Greeting and Hierarchy
story.arc: Exposition of dharma regarding respectful address and relative status among different classes and roles.
story.timeline_note: Timeless legal instruction; no narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: When greeting a superior, one should state one's name and say 'asau nāmāham asmi' (I am so-and-so).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this practice of humble self-identification aligns with the principle of egolessness and surrender to the guru.

teachings.teaching: Those who do not know the proper greeting are to be considered like śūdras and not worthy of being greeted by the wise.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that ignorance of proper conduct reflects lack of inner discipline; Kriya-Yoga stresses the importance of correct action (kriya) for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa of ten years and a king of a hundred years: the brāhmaṇa is to be regarded as father, and the brāhmaṇa is the father of both.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the supremacy of spiritual knowledge over temporal power, a key tenet in Kriya-Yoga where the guru's wisdom transcends worldly status.

teachings.teaching: The ācārya who teaches the Veda with its auxiliaries is greater than ten upādhyāyas, a hundred ācāryas, and even the father; the mother exceeds a thousand fathers in reverence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would see this as reinforcing the guru's role as the ultimate guide in Kriya-Yoga, while also honoring the mother's nurturing aspect.

teachings.teaching: The giver of spiritual birth (brahmada) is more venerable than the biological father; spiritual birth is eternal both here and hereafter.
teachings.lens_note: This directly parallels the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on initiation (diksha) as a second birth, where the guru becomes the true parent.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti discusses the relative importance of the spiritual teacher (ācārya) versus the biological father, emphasizing that the teacher who imparts Vedic knowledge is the true father of a twice-born. It also covers the qualities of a learned brāhmaṇa, the importance of Vedic study, and the consequences of neglecting it.

story.title: The True Father and the Importance of Vedic Study
story.arc: Didactic exposition on the guru's role and the duty of a brāhmaṇa to study the Vedas.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; part of the Dharmaśāstra tradition.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily instructional.

teachings.teaching: The teacher who imparts Veda is greater than the biological father, as spiritual birth is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the guru's initiation (dīkṣā) is the true birth into spiritual life, surpassing physical lineage.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa who neglects Vedic study becomes like a wooden elephant or a leather deer—only in name.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the necessity of continuous practice (abhyāsa) in Kriya-Yoga; without it, one is spiritually dead.

teachings.teaching: One should speak gently and truthfully, avoiding harsh words even when distressed.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the yama of satya and ahimsa in Kriya-Yoga; speech must be purified to support inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa should always fear honor like poison and desire disrespect like nectar.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as detachment from ego; in Kriya-Yoga, humility is essential for progress.

teachings.teaching: Vedic study is the highest austerity for a brāhmaṇa.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, svādhyāya (self-study) is a key niyama; this verse reinforces that consistent study is a form of tapas.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Manusmriti (verses 2.170-2.194) details the duties and regulations for a brahmacārī (celibate student) after the upanayana (sacred thread ceremony). It covers the role of the teacher as father, the student's daily practices including begging for alms, restrictions on food and behavior, and the importance of serving the guru.

story.title: Duties of a Brahmacārī
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: After upanayana, during Vedic study period
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily prescriptive text.

teachings.teaching: The teacher is the father and Sāvitrī is the mother of the twice-born student.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the guru as the spiritual parent who initiates the student into the inner yogic path, beyond biological lineage.

teachings.teaching: Until one is born through the Veda (upanayana), they are equal to a śūdra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the necessity of spiritual rebirth through initiation and practice; without it, one remains in ignorance.

teachings.teaching: The brahmacārī should control the senses, bathe daily, perform offerings to gods, sages, and ancestors, and worship deities.
teachings.lens_note: These external disciplines are preparatory for internal Kriya-Yoga practices, purifying the body and mind for higher meditation.

teachings.teaching: The student must avoid honey, meat, perfumes, garlands, women, sour substances, and harming living beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees these as aids to reduce sensory distractions and maintain brahmacarya (celibacy), essential for conserving energy for yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: The brahmacārī should sleep alone and avoid emission of semen; if it happens involuntarily in sleep, he should bathe and chant a mantra.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the importance of ojas (vital energy) preservation, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga for spiritual advancement.

teachings.teaching: Begging for alms daily is prescribed; the student should not eat from a single house and should avoid certain families.
teachings.lens_note: Alms-begging cultivates humility and detachment, aligning with the yogic ideal of non-attachment to material comforts.

teachings.teaching: The student should gather firewood and perform fire offerings morning and evening without laziness.
teachings.lens_note: The fire offerings symbolize inner purification and the kindling of the inner fire (agni) through Kriya practices.

teachings.teaching: If the student fails to beg or offer fire for seven days without illness, he must perform an expiatory vow.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of regularity in spiritual practice; lapses require atonement to restore discipline.

teachings.teaching: The student should always be eager to study and serve the teacher, controlling body, speech, and mind.
teachings.lens_note: Service to the guru is a form of karma yoga, preparing the student for higher teachings of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 2.194-2.218) provides detailed rules of conduct for a student (brahmacārin) towards his guru (teacher) and the guru's family. It prescribes respectful behavior, including posture, speech, and avoidance of disrespect, and emphasizes the importance of controlling senses, especially around women. The chapter also discusses the proper way to greet and serve the guru, and the consequences of slander.

story.title: Conduct Towards the Guru
story.arc: Instruction on proper behavior for a student towards his teacher and the teacher's family, emphasizing humility, service, and control of senses.
story.timeline_note: Part of the larger Manusmriti text, likely composed between 200 BCE and 200 CE.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is didactic and prescriptive, lacking narrative events or dramatic tension.

teachings.teaching: A student should always be humble and respectful in the presence of his guru, rising first and sitting last.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this external discipline mirrors internal surrender to the inner guru (consciousness), essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should not address the guru while lying down, sitting, eating, or standing with one's back turned.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes mindful presence; in Kriya-Yoga, every action is a form of meditation, requiring full attention.

teachings.teaching: When the guru is seated, the student should stand; when the guru approaches, the student should go to meet him.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the student's active engagement and readiness to receive wisdom, akin to the practitioner's alertness in meditation.

teachings.teaching: One should not imitate the guru's gait, speech, or gestures.
teachings.lens_note: True respect involves honoring the guru's uniqueness, not superficial mimicry; in Kriya-Yoga, one must internalize the essence, not just the form.

teachings.teaching: If there is slander or criticism of the guru, one should cover one's ears or leave the place.
teachings.lens_note: Protecting the mind from negativity is crucial; in Kriya-Yoga, the mind must be shielded from distractions to maintain inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: One who slanders the guru becomes a donkey, a dog, a worm, or an insect in future lives.
teachings.lens_note: This karmic consequence underscores the gravity of disrespect; in Kriya-Yoga, the guru is the conduit to the divine, and disrespect disrupts spiritual connection.

teachings.teaching: A student should not sit alone with the guru's wife, daughter, or sister, as the senses are powerful and can lead even the wise astray.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the need for vigilance over the senses; in Kriya-Yoga, sense control (pratyahara) is foundational for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: By serving the guru with dedication, one attains knowledge, just as water is found by digging.
teachings.lens_note: Service (seva) is a form of karma yoga that purifies the mind; in Kriya-Yoga, selfless service prepares the practitioner for higher states.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 2.218-2.242) discusses the importance of reverence and service to one's teacher (guru), mother, and father, equating them with the three sacred fires and the three worlds. It emphasizes that true penance lies in serving these three, and that their satisfaction fulfills all religious duties. The text also advises acquiring knowledge and virtue from any source, even from a lower caste or an enemy, in times of need.

story.title: Reverence to Guru, Mother, and Father
story.arc: Didactic exposition on the duties of a student and householder
story.timeline_note: Timeless; part of Manusmriti's instructions on dharma
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily instructional text

teachings.teaching: Knowledge is obtained through service to the teacher, just as water is obtained by digging.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the necessity of disciplined effort (kriya) and surrender to the guru as the means to access higher knowledge.

teachings.teaching: One should not let the sun rise or set while lying down; if it happens, one should fast and chant.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on aligning daily routines with natural cycles and using pranayama (chanting) to purify the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: One should perform sandhya (twilight worship) with purity and concentration.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views sandhya as a key practice for balancing the pranic energies and connecting with the cosmic rhythm.

teachings.teaching: The three aims of life (dharma, artha, kama) are to be pursued, but dharma is supreme.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, dharma is the foundation for spiritual evolution; artha and kama are acceptable only when aligned with dharma.

teachings.teaching: One should never disrespect mother, father, teacher, or elder brother, even in distress.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these relationships are external manifestations of the divine; honoring them purifies the heart and accelerates spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: The teacher is the form of Brahma, father of Prajapati, mother of Earth, and elder brother of one's own self.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the non-dual vision in Kriya-Yoga: all beings are expressions of the one Self.

teachings.teaching: The debt to parents cannot be repaid even in a hundred years; one should always please them and the teacher.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the necessity of gratitude and selfless service (karma yoga) as a means to dissolve the ego.

teachings.teaching: Service to these three (mother, father, teacher) is the highest penance; without their permission, one should not undertake other dharmas.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this highlights that the foundation of all spiritual practice is harmonious relationships and humility.

teachings.teaching: These three are the three worlds, the three ashramas, the three Vedas, and the three fires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a microcosm-macrocosm correspondence: the family is a miniature universe, and serving them is serving the cosmos.

teachings.teaching: By not neglecting these three, a householder conquers the three worlds and shines like a god.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's goal of self-realization through disciplined household life, not renunciation.

teachings.teaching: Through devotion to mother, one gains this world; to father, the middle world; to teacher, the Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a progressive purification of the subtle body, leading to higher states of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: All duties are fulfilled if these three are honored; otherwise, all actions are fruitless.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the Kriya-Yoga principle that inner attitude (bhava) is more important than external rituals.

teachings.teaching: As long as these three live, one should not undertake other duties; one should serve them with pleasure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the primary sadhana (spiritual practice) is selfless service to one's immediate elders.

teachings.teaching: Whatever one does for the hereafter should be done without obstructing them, and should be offered to them through thought, word, and deed.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching integrates karma yoga and bhakti yoga: all actions are offerings to the divine in the form of parents and teacher.

teachings.teaching: The entire purpose of a person is fulfilled in these three; this is the direct dharma, all else is secondary.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga considers this the essence of dharma: living in harmony with one's immediate relationships as a path to liberation.

teachings.teaching: One should acquire knowledge and virtue even from a lower person, and a gem of a woman even from a low family.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of humility and openness to learning from all sources, transcending social barriers.

teachings.teaching: One should take nectar even from poison, good speech even from a child, good conduct even from an enemy, and gold even from impure sources.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes discernment (viveka) and the ability to extract the essence from any situation, a key skill in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Women, gems, knowledge, dharma, purity, good speech, and various arts should be accepted from all sources.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching encourages an inclusive approach to learning and acquiring virtues, aligning with the universal outlook of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: In times of distress, one may study under a non-Brahmana teacher, but should not stay permanently with a non-Brahmana or an unlearned Brahmana.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a practical guideline for maintaining spiritual purity while adapting to circumstances.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 2.242-3.17) discusses the duties of a brahmacārin (celibate student) towards his guru, the transition to householder life, and rules for marriage, including suitable brides and prohibited family lines.

story.title: Duties of a Student and Householder
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Timeless legal and moral code.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text.

teachings.teaching: A student should not reside permanently with a non-Brahmin teacher, but may stay with a learned Brahmin teacher seeking ultimate liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of choosing a guru who embodies pure consciousness and can guide the student beyond the ego.

teachings.teaching: If a student desires permanent residence in the teacher's house, he should serve the teacher diligently until death.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of total surrender (prapatti) to the guru as a means to transcend the limited self.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmin who serves his teacher until the end of his life attains the eternal abode of Brahma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this signifies that unwavering devotion to the guru leads to realization of the Self (Brahman).

teachings.teaching: A student should not give anything to the teacher before completing his studies; after being permitted to bathe, he may bring gifts according to his ability.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the purity of the student-teacher relationship, where the student first receives knowledge without transactional expectations.

teachings.teaching: Gifts like land, gold, cow, horse, umbrella, shoes, seat, grain, vegetables, and clothes please the teacher.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these offerings symbolize the student's gratitude and the material support necessary for the teacher's spiritual work.

teachings.teaching: After the teacher's death, the student should treat the teacher's son (if qualified), wife, or close relative as the teacher.
teachings.lens_note: This maintains the continuity of the guru-parampara (lineage), a key concept in Kriya-Yoga for preserving spiritual transmission.

teachings.teaching: If none of these are available, the student should engage in self-study and serve the sacred fire to perfect his body.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on self-discipline and inner purification through practices like pranayama and meditation.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmin who completes his celibate studentship without lapse attains the highest state and is not reborn.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to achieving kaivalya (liberation) through sustained practice and celibacy (brahmacharya).

teachings.teaching: The Vedic studentship may last 36 years, half that, a quarter, or until the student has learned the Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: This flexibility acknowledges individual capacity, a principle Kriya-Yoga applies in tailoring practices to the student's readiness.

teachings.teaching: After studying the Vedas without breaking celibacy, the student should enter the householder stage.
teachings.lens_note: This transition mirrors the Kriya-Yoga path where spiritual practice continues in active life, integrating meditation with worldly duties.

teachings.teaching: A householder should honor a guest who is learned and has completed his studentship, offering him a cow as the first gift.
teachings.lens_note: Hospitality is a form of seva (selfless service), which in Kriya-Yoga purifies the heart and reduces ego.

teachings.teaching: With the teacher's permission, a twice-born man should marry a wife of equal caste and with auspicious marks.
teachings.lens_note: Marriage in Kriya-Yoga is seen as a partnership for spiritual growth, not just social duty.

teachings.teaching: A bride should not be a sapinda of the mother nor of the same gotra as the father; such marriages are praised for twice-born.
teachings.lens_note: These rules ensure genetic diversity and maintain social harmony, which supports a stable environment for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Ten families to avoid for marriage: those neglecting rituals, without male children, without Vedic study, with hairy/hemorrhoidal disease, consumption, epilepsy, white leprosy, or black leprosy.
teachings.lens_note: These prohibitions aim to avoid hereditary diseases and ensure a healthy lineage, important for sustained spiritual discipline.

teachings.teaching: Do not marry a girl who is red-haired, has an extra limb, is sick, hairless, too hairy, talkative, or with yellow eyes.
teachings.lens_note: These physical characteristics were considered inauspicious; in Kriya-Yoga, external purity reflects inner harmony.

teachings.teaching: Avoid girls named after stars, trees, rivers, low mountains, birds, snakes, or servants, or with frightening names.
teachings.lens_note: Names carry vibrational energy; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the power of sound (mantra) and thus names are chosen carefully.

teachings.teaching: Marry a girl with no bodily defect, a pleasant name, a gait like a swan or elephant, fine hair, teeth, and soft limbs.
teachings.lens_note: These qualities indicate a balanced constitution, conducive to a harmonious household and spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Do not marry a girl whose brother or father is unknown, due to suspicion of being a putrikā (appointed daughter).
teachings.lens_note: Clarity of lineage ensures social stability, which supports the dharma of the householder.

teachings.teaching: For twice-born, a wife of equal caste is recommended; if marrying lower castes, the order is: Kshatriya, Vaishya, Shudra.
teachings.lens_note: This hierarchy reflects the varna system; in Kriya-Yoga, one's social duties (svadharma) are a stepping stone to transcendence.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmin or Kshatriya should never marry a Shudra woman, even in distress.
teachings.lens_note: This strict rule maintains caste purity, which in traditional view supports the preservation of dharma.

teachings.teaching: A twice-born who marries a lower-caste woman degrades his family to Shudra status quickly.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against actions that disrupt social order, which is seen as essential for collective spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Examples: Atri, Utathya, Śaunaka, and Bhṛgu fell due to marrying a Shudra woman.
teachings.lens_note: These historical precedents reinforce the gravity of the rule; in Kriya-Yoga, adherence to dharma is crucial for spiritual ascent.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmin who sleeps with a Shudra woman goes to hell; begetting a son on her makes him lose his Brahminhood.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the severe consequences of violating caste boundaries, which disrupts the social and spiritual order.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 3, verses 17-41) discusses the consequences of a Brahmana marrying a Shudra woman, the eight types of marriage (Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, and Paishacha), their appropriateness for different varnas, and the qualities of offspring resulting from each type.

story.title: Eight Types of Marriage and Their Consequences
story.arc: Exposition of dharma regarding marriages, their classification, and the qualities of offspring.
story.timeline_note: Part of Manusmriti, a foundational text of Dharmaśāstra.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic legal text.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmana who sleeps with a Shudra woman goes to hell; if he begets a son with her, he falls from Brahminhood.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes purity of lineage and the importance of maintaining one's sattvic nature through appropriate associations.

teachings.teaching: The eight types of marriage are: Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, and Paishacha.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as stages of spiritual evolution in relationships, from the highest (Brahma) to the lowest (Paishacha), reflecting the quality of consciousness involved.

teachings.teaching: For a Brahmana, the first four marriages (Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya) are praiseworthy; for a Kshatriya, the Rakshasa type is also allowed; for Vaishya and Shudra, the Asura type is allowed.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that different paths suit different temperaments and stages of spiritual development.

teachings.teaching: The Paishacha and Asura marriages should never be performed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against unions based on base instincts, which hinder spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Gandharva and Rakshasa marriages are considered lawful for Kshatriyas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see these as reflecting the warrior's nature, where passion and force can be channeled for dharma.

teachings.teaching: Offspring from Brahma marriage liberate ten ancestors, ten descendants, and oneself; from Daiva marriage, seven each; from Arsha, three each; from Prajapatya, six each.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would view this as the karmic impact of the marriage type on the lineage, emphasizing the importance of conscious choice.

teachings.teaching: Children born from the first four marriages are radiant, wealthy, righteous, and live a hundred years.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle that sattvic unions produce sattvic offspring, a key concept in Kriya-Yoga for spiritual heredity.

teachings.teaching: Children from the remaining (blameworthy) marriages are cruel, untruthful, and hate dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a warning against tamasic and rajasic unions that degrade consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti discusses rules for marriage, emphasizing the importance of marrying within one's varna, avoiding improper marriages, and the consequences of bad marriages. It also covers the treatment of women, stating that where women are honored, the gods are pleased, and where they are not, all actions become fruitless. The chapter includes guidelines for sexual relations during the wife's fertile period and the importance of mutual satisfaction between husband and wife.

story.title: Marriage and Family Ethics
story.arc: Exposition of dharma regarding marriage, treatment of women, and family prosperity.
story.timeline_note: Part of Manusmriti, a foundational legal text of ancient India.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily prescriptive and legalistic.

teachings.teaching: Marriage should be within one's own varna; improper marriages produce offspring who hate dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of purity of lineage and environment for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Women should be honored and adorned; where women are honored, gods are pleased.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a practical application of bhakti and respect for the divine feminine, essential for householder yoga.

teachings.teaching: A husband and wife should be mutually content; if the wife is not pleased, the husband will not prosper.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on harmony in relationships as a foundation for kriya-yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Avoid improper marriages, neglect of rituals, and lack of Vedic study to prevent family decline.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as maintaining sattvic habits and discipline for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 3, verses 65-89) discusses the five great sacrifices (pañca-mahāyajña) that a householder must perform daily: Brahma-yajña (teaching/study), Pitṛ-yajña (offerings to ancestors), Deva-yajña (offerings to gods), Bhūta-yajña (offerings to beings), and Nṛ-yajṇa (hospitality to guests). It emphasizes that these sacrifices purify the householder from the inevitable sins of domestic life and sustain the entire social order. The text details the procedures, including homa (offerings in fire) and bali (offerings to various deities and directions).

story.title: The Five Great Sacrifices of the Householder
story.arc: Exposition of dharma for householders
story.timeline_note: Timeless instruction; part of Manusmriti's dharmaśāstra.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: The householder must perform five great sacrifices daily: Brahma-yajña (study/teaching), Pitṛ-yajña (offerings to ancestors), Deva-yajña (offerings to gods), Bhūta-yajña (offerings to beings), and Nṛ-yajña (hospitality to guests).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these five sacrifices represent the integration of inner spiritual practice (svādhyāya) with outer service, purifying the five senses and aligning the householder with cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: By performing these sacrifices, the householder is not tainted by the inevitable sins of domestic life (sūnādoṣa).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the transformative power of intentional action (kriyā) that transmutes karmic residues into spiritual merit.

teachings.teaching: The householder sustains all other āśramas (stages of life) and is therefore the most excellent āśrama.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on active engagement in the world as a path to liberation, not renunciation alone.

teachings.teaching: Offerings to gods and beings should be made with proper mantras and procedures, including homa and bali to various deities.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these rituals as external aids to focus the mind and invoke divine energies, but the essence is the inner attitude of offering.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (Chapter 3, verses 89-113) details the rituals of the Vaiśvadeva offering and the duties of a householder towards guests (atithi). It prescribes offerings to deities, beings, and ancestors, followed by hospitality rules for brāhmaṇas and other guests, emphasizing the spiritual benefits of honoring guests.

story.title: Vaiśvadeva and Atithi Hospitality
story.arc: Prescriptive ritual and ethical duties for householders
story.timeline_note: Timeless injunctions; no narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: Offerings should be made to deities, beings, and ancestors as part of the Vaiśvadeva ritual.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the integration of all levels of existence into daily practice, harmonizing the inner and outer worlds.

teachings.teaching: Leftover offerings should be given to dogs, fallen persons, and other creatures.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of non-waste and compassion, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on selfless action and awareness of interconnectedness.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa who honors all beings attains the supreme state.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the realization of unity consciousness through disciplined action (kriya).

teachings.teaching: Guests (atithi) must be honored with food and hospitality according to one's capacity.
teachings.lens_note: Hospitality is a form of yoga, training the mind to serve without attachment, a core Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Giving alms to a learned brāhmaṇa yields the same merit as giving a cow to a guru.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of supporting spiritual seekers, which in Kriya-Yoga includes supporting one's own inner practice.

teachings.teaching: Offerings to unworthy recipients are wasted.
teachings.lens_note: Discernment (viveka) is essential; Kriya-Yoga teaches that energy should be directed wisely.

teachings.teaching: A guest who stays one night is a brāhmaṇa; the term 'atithi' derives from 'anitya' (impermanent).
teachings.lens_note: This reminds of the transient nature of all encounters, encouraging mindfulness in every interaction.

teachings.teaching: A householder should not eat before feeding a guest.
teachings.lens_note: Self-restraint and prioritization of others' needs are key virtues in Kriya-Yoga's ethical foundation.

teachings.teaching: Guests should be treated according to their status, with appropriate respect.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches adaptability and humility, qualities cultivated in Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: After the Vaiśvadeva offering, if another guest arrives, feed him but do not repeat the offering.
teachings.lens_note: Rituals have structure; spontaneity within discipline is a Kriya-Yoga principle.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa should not announce his lineage for food; doing so is condemned.
teachings.lens_note: Humility and avoidance of self-promotion are essential; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes inner purity over external status.

teachings.teaching: Only a brāhmaṇa is considered a guest for a brāhmaṇa householder; others are not guests in the same sense.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the importance of spiritual hierarchy in traditional contexts, though Kriya-Yoga may reinterpret it as inner hierarchy of values.

teachings.teaching: If a kṣatriya arrives as a guest, he may be fed after the brāhmaṇas have eaten.
teachings.lens_note: Order and precedence in hospitality mirror the orderliness of yogic practice.

teachings.teaching: Vaiśyas and śūdras arriving as guests should be fed with servants, out of compassion.
teachings.lens_note: Compassion extends to all, regardless of caste; Kriya-Yoga sees all as manifestations of the same consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Other friends and relatives should be fed with one's wife, according to capacity.
teachings.lens_note: Inclusiveness and sharing are part of the householder's dharma, reflecting the Kriya-Yoga ideal of harmonious living.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 3.113-3.137) provides detailed rules for householder hospitality, including feeding guests, relatives, and performing offerings to gods and ancestors. It emphasizes the importance of feeding learned Brahmins, the order of serving, and the consequences of improper conduct.

story.title: Rules for Householder Hospitality and Offerings
story.arc: Dharmaśāstra exposition on householder duties
story.timeline_note: Timeless prescriptive text; no narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic legal text.

teachings.teaching: Feed guests, relatives, and dependents before oneself; the householder and wife eat last.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of selfless action (niṣkāma karma) and service to others as a form of purification.

teachings.teaching: One who cooks only for himself eats sin; the remnants of a sacrifice are the food of the virtuous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that all actions should be offered as a sacrifice to the divine, transforming egoistic acts into spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Honor a king, priest, Vedic graduate, teacher, father-in-law, and maternal uncle with madhuparka once a year.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches respect for authority and tradition, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga value of humility and reverence.

teachings.teaching: The wife should offer a bali (offering) to the household deities without mantras in the evening.
teachings.lens_note: This domestic ritual symbolizes the integration of spiritual practice into daily life, a core Kriya-Yoga principle.

teachings.teaching: Perform monthly śrāddha offerings to ancestors on the new moon day.
teachings.lens_note: Honoring ancestors is seen as a way to maintain continuity and gratitude, supporting the yogic path of detachment with responsibility.

teachings.teaching: Feed a learned Brahmin (śrotriya) at śrāddha; even one such Brahmin brings great merit.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of knowledge and purity in receiving offerings, reflecting the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on inner purity.

teachings.teaching: An ignorant man who partakes in offerings without mantras suffers in the afterlife.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against mechanical ritual without understanding, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's call for conscious practice.

teachings.teaching: Offer kavya (ancestral offerings) to Brahmins who are jñāna-niṣṭha (established in knowledge), and havya (offerings to gods) to all four types.
teachings.lens_note: This distinguishes between types of offerings and recipients, teaching discernment in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: If the father is ignorant but the son is learned, the father is considered superior; if the son is ignorant but the father learned, the father is still superior.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the value of lineage and tradition, yet also the importance of personal attainment.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti (verses 3.137-3.161) provides detailed rules for who should be invited and fed at śrāddha ceremonies, emphasizing the importance of inviting learned Brahmins and avoiding those who are unworthy due to various sins or defects.

story.title: Rules for Śrāddha Invitations
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Part of the Dharmaśāstra tradition, not a narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low

teachings.teaching: One should not feed a friend at a śrāddha; rather, one should feed a Brahmin who is neither a friend nor an enemy.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes detachment and impartiality in sacred acts, aligning with the principle of non-attachment to outcomes.

teachings.teaching: Feeding only friends at śrāddha yields no fruit in the afterlife.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against ego-driven actions; true spiritual merit comes from selfless service.

teachings.teaching: One who ignorantly makes friends through śrāddha falls from heaven.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the Kriya-Yoga teaching that actions should be performed without personal desire, as desire binds one to samsara.

teachings.teaching: A gift to an ignorant person is like sowing seed in barren soil; it yields no fruit.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a call to discernment: spiritual practices must be directed properly to be effective.

teachings.teaching: One should invite a learned Brahmin who has mastered the Vedas, such as a Bahvṛca, Adhvaryu, or Chandoga.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on knowledge and practice; only those who embody the teachings can transmit spiritual benefit.

teachings.teaching: Feeding such a learned Brahmin brings eternal satisfaction to one's ancestors for seven generations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might note that this reflects the power of true knowledge to liberate not just oneself but one's lineage.

teachings.teaching: One should not examine a Brahmin for divine rites, but for ancestral rites one should examine carefully.
teachings.lens_note: This distinction highlights the Kriya-Yoga principle that different practices require different levels of purity and intention.

teachings.teaching: Many types of unworthy Brahmins are listed, including thieves, eunuchs, atheists, and those with physical defects.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a guide to avoid associating with those who hinder spiritual progress; purity of association is important in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Manusmriti lists various types of people who are unfit to be invited to śrāddha feasts (apāṅkteya), including those with physical deformities, immoral professions, or improper conduct. It also discusses the consequences of feeding such individuals and identifies those who purify the line (paṅktipāvana).

story.title: Rules for Invitees to Śrāddha
story.arc: Exposition of who should be excluded from and who purifies the sacred line of guests at a śrāddha ceremony.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Dharmaśāstra tradition, not a narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of rules with no dramatic action.

teachings.teaching: Certain persons are unfit to be invited to śrāddha: those with diseases, immoral occupations, or improper conduct.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would emphasize purity of mind and action; inviting such persons reflects inner impurity and disrupts the spiritual efficacy of the ritual.

teachings.teaching: Feeding an unworthy person nullifies the merit of the offering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning that actions performed with impure intent or association yield no spiritual fruit; the inner state of the giver and receiver matters.

teachings.teaching: Those who are learned in the Vedas and of good lineage purify the line of guests.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as highlighting the importance of knowledge and virtuous lineage as conduits for spiritual energy; such individuals embody the Kriya-Yoga ideal of disciplined practice.
